item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Redacting the numbers ruins the ending. "Oh no, the mystery blackbox numbers changed a little!" By how much? How severe is the change? I don't know, the ratio was redacted the entire time. Was it a small change, not too noticeable but somewhat significant, or was it a major change, with world changing values? I don't know. And therefore the ending is just an entire missing because I simply don't actually know how serious it is, with the range being anywhere from "very very minor" to "nigh-apocalyptic".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may have somewhat misunderstood the point you were making then, and I generally agree with what you're saying here. In additional to leading readers away, crosslinks also make people feel like they're going to miss out on something if they don't read them (or already know the contents).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think we're more in agreement than it seems at first glance.

There are two parts to my suggestion for improved technobabble. The executive summary is vital of course. But just as important is limiting the length of the initial spiel.

TNG kept it to a half dozen sentences of babble. That seems a bit too restrictive in this case, but shorter than the current version definitely seems called for.

Tangent: I have a half-formed theory that crosslinks lead to less voting. Not because (or not only because) people don't like them. But because it leads readers off to other pages and some percentage of them won't come back to vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I doubt technobabble flavor is the prime reason for the article's middling rating. Ending on "and then some guy broke it" wasn't particularly satisfying for me, especially after the amount of buildup that went into the whole thing. Terse punchlines suit shorter articles more, or else it can feel like a betrayal of reader expectations. The crosslinks drag things out too, the interview didn't add much in my eyes, etc.

Not commenting on whether or not a sentence or two of executive summary would improve things, but I think it requires some thorough revision regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation broke a fundamental constant! And is trying to cover it up like a kid with the shards of mom's favorite lamp.
I was shocked to see this was only +5, but then I paused for a second and realized why.

The technobabble is strong with this one, and I love that. Particularly because there is no "babble" involved. The fact that 80/20 shows up so often irl is weird, and more than one academic has made a career of studying some particular instance of it.

But most of the readership here prefer their technobabble ST:TNG style. No more than six sentences followed by a one sentence analogy that a child or commanding officer could understand.

So how to tweak this so that the average wiki-member can appreciate it?
I'd say start by not censoring the ratio itself. The slow reveal will still happen because most people won't realize the significance of the variation from 80/20 until they've read further. I see upthread that you had good in-universe reasons for the redaction, but this seems like one of those times where the realities of writing fiction need to take precedent over in-universe consistency.

I also don't see any equivalent of that Star Trek executive analogy. One more sentence at the end of the description along the lines of "SCP-1822 proves that we live in a "lucky" universe, and determines exactly how much the odds are in humanity's favor." That would get the point across to folks whose eyes glazed over at "axioms of mathematical statistics", and also underline the punchline of how catastrophic it is when they screw it up later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I have clarified and cleaned up most of the issues.

It is a probabilistic phenomenon, they're chance of outcomes based on two generally unrelated numbers. It no longer says ratio.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But… Wouldn't something that was 50:50 just be 1:1?

Also, I get kind of squinty when I see ratios that aren't whole numbers. I mean, if one of the numbers involved is root 2 or something, sure, but I don't think any mathematician would write something like 30.6:82.8. They'd make sure at least one of those numbers (probably the one on the right) was a whole number.

Unless I have completely misinterpreted what was meant by "ratio" in this context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, if you were reading documents scrubbed for the public, it would probably be large swaths of redactions anyway. This is pretty mild considering, but I'll probably change it and do a re-write. Imo I don't think it would seem narratively fruitful to give away the number simply because you understand from context- it was decently okay, now it's worse. The parieto principle is 80:20, but the values have been modified by the Foundation, it's probably similar

I guess I just don't see what a person could gleam from that information that isn't revealed in other ways. It's simply classified or non-essential information. The containment procedures talk about limiting the knowledge of the extent of its properties. That's what they are doing. Staff don't need to know, the public don't need to know. Only if you were involved in the project would you be made aware of the actual value.

That's how I see it. I'll see how it looks though if I replace the unicode blocks with the actual number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this an SCP that causes all numbers to vanish?

item.title: The flaws of the article
item.content:encoded: To me, the main flaws on this article are the poorly-worded containment procedures (they really need a rewrite. What even is "Euclid-Red"?) and the experiment project. I get what this SCP is trying to do, but it doesn't do it well. Down voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree.
I certainly enjoyed this, but while I also "get" what is happening, the ambiguity of it feels like a math question, where a teacher said:
"Try to solve this, maybe it has a solution. I'm gonna have a smoke outside."

Not a situation, where I personally enjoy myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good point, it certainly isn't helping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel as though the anomaly's effects are valueless in the context of the article because almost all of the actual numbers are redacted for no clear reason. The anomaly is based on numbers and we don't get to see any of them. The Foundation quantified luck — that's the only thing I get from this, and there's a lot of article here to walk away with only that.

I'm not a math whiz by any stretch, but to me, this wasn't mathematically dense because everything that could have been dense is redacted. ]:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Low vote count and lack of comments? I feel this. But the fact is, this is really mathematically dense, and by the end, I'm not really sure what's going on. You might need to go back to the drawing board. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something that I've had for a while and never got around to posting. Hope this is liked! It's based on the Pareto Principle.
